text: Behavior of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ in a Hypothetical CTR 3

Breeding of tritium in a molten-salt blanket is at best marginal when the lithium is in its natural isotopic abundance; however, the tritium-breeding ratio can be improved by including blanket regions of lithium or of beryllium, or by enriching the salt in lithium-6.

While fluorides are adequate heat-transfer agents and possess good radiation stability, neutronic transmutation of Be and F in the salt can lead to corrosion unless a redox buffer (analogous to $\mathrm{U}^{3+}/\mathrm{U}^{4+}$ in a fission reactor) is included in the melt.
For example, LiCl-KCl, melting at $354^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , may be adequate as vacuum wall coolant and as the fluid to transport heat to the steam system of the reactor.
A controlled thermonuclear reactor (CTR) that fuses deuterons with tritons yields 17.6 MeV per fusion event mostly in the form of very energetic neutrons.

Though these two functions are separable in principle,[1] there is probably a considerable advantage, other than elegance, if the coolant fluid can be a liquid with sufficient lithium to sustain the required tritium production.
These are generally similar to the requirements imposed on molten salts as fuels for fission reactor fuels (1).

text: To illustrate the nature and general magnitude of the problems we have chosen a toroidal CTR, blanketed and cooled with molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ , of the size and characteristics shown in Table 1.

text: These assumptions, as to pumping rates, $\Delta T$ , and steam generation compatibility, are not unlike those proposed for molten salt breeder (fission) reactors (MSBRs) (4).

Molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ (or any conducting fluid) flowing at 10 meters/second in a pipe of 5 cm diameter with its axis aligned perpendicular to the lines of force of an 80 kgauss (8 volt-sec/meter²) magnetic field will have induced, at right angles to both the magnetic field and the
flow direction, a potential difference of 4 volts between the salt and the pipe wall.

Potential differences of such magnitudes are clearly intolerable; though LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ are both very stable compounds (6) an induced voltage such as this (equivalent to destabilization by 92 kcal/mole) would make these compounds quite corrosive to the metallic tube walls.

Homeyer (7), who seems to have been the first to consider such electrolytic corrosion in a CTR blanket system, noted that such corrosion should be alleviated by (a) reducing fluid velocities perpendicular to the magnetic field, and (b) using a series of parallel pipes to reduce the pipe dimension where flow across the magnetic field lines are necessary.

If, for example, the 120 ft³/sec flow of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ required of our hypothetical 1000 MW(e) CTR were supplied at a flow rate of 4 meters/sec through pipes of 4 cm diameter perpendicular to the 25 kgauss field³ the emf induced in each pipe would be 0.4 volts; some 675 pipes would be required.
If these pipes penetrated the field at $30^{\circ}$ to the field lines this emf would be reduced to 0.2 volts.
Chemical effects of this poloidal field (which may reach 7 kgauss) may not be trivial, but they would seem, in general, to be tolerable.
No experimental study of magnetic damping in molten salt has been reported, but experiments with liquid mercury have been performed (8,9).

In the absence of a magnetic field the laminar-turbulent transition occurs at Reynolds number $(\mathbb{R}_0)$ of about 2200; in the presence of the field this transition occurs at a higher Reynolds number $(\mathbb{R}_{t})$ .
This increase (more commonly the ratio $(\mathbb{R}_{t} / \mathbb{R}_{0})$ is a function of the Hartman number (M).
Three such correlation functions have been published (8,9,10).
For $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the electrical conductivity (11) is 220 (ohm-meter) $^{-1}$ , the viscosity is $8 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{kg/(sec-meter)}$ .
Accordingly, for this material in a 80 kgauss (8 webers/meter $^2$ ) field and assuming a 6 cm (3 cm half-width) channel the value of the Hartman number is 40.
If we apply each of the three correlations (8,9,10) developed from experiments with mercury to a fluid of

As an approximation let us assume that to cool the vacuum wall we require 40 ft³ per second of salt (one third the quantity required to cool the entire blanket) and that this salt flows through a 6 cm wide annulus around the 7 meter diameter first wall.
The linear velocity of salt is 0.85 meters/sec, the density (11) (at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) of $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{BeF}_{4}$ is 1990 kg/meter³ and the viscosity is 8 centipoise.
The Reynolds number under these conditions is about 13,000.
Hoffman and Carlson (10) propose the formula, $\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{t}} = 500\mathbf{M}$ for calculating the transition Reynolds number of Mercury flowing transverse to the magnetic field.
The same formula applied to $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ flowing in a 6-cm thick channel transverse to 8 kgauss (the poloidal field strength) yields

This result suggests that an 8 kgauss poloidal field in a toroidal reactor may not affect turbulence in $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ ; at most, the suppression of turbulence by this field will be comparable to the effects of the toroidal field.
If the only sources of tritium were today's transmutation facilities then the fuel cost alone would be about 1.2 cents per kilowatt-hour.[6]

The only practical neutron reactions which will yield tritium sufficient for the needs of a D-T fusion reactor are $^6\mathrm{Li}(\mathfrak{n},\alpha)\mathfrak{T}$ and $^7\mathrm{Li}(\mathfrak{n},\alpha\mathfrak{n}^{\prime})\mathfrak{T}$ .
The latter is a high-energy reaction with a threshold at 2.5 MeV.
Neutron-capture cross sections of $^6\mathrm{Li}$ become significant only at energies of 0.5 MeV and less.

The $^7\mathrm{Li}$ reaction is particularly favorable since the product neutron $(\mathfrak{n}^{\prime})$ can react with $^6\mathrm{Li}$ to yield a second tritium atom, but because the blanket contains elements which scatter and absorb high-energy neutrons, the production of tritium from $^7\mathrm{Li}$ is not very efficient.
When the lithium in the blanket is in natural isotopic abundance (92.58%, $^7\mathrm{Li}$ , 7.42%, $^6\mathrm{Li}$ ), the greater fraction of tritium is produced from $^6\mathrm{Li}$ .

(c) modest enrichment of the blanket material in $^6\mathrm{Li}$ .

The second option was briefly treated by Bell (16) who showed that if a blanket region (40 cm thick adjoining a 1 cm first wall of molybdenum) were changed from $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ to an equal thickness of Be and $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ , the tritium-breeding ratio would increase from 0.95 to 1.50.
Impink (13) reported that small increases in $^6\mathrm{Li}$ enrichment of the $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ blanket led to modest gains in breeding ratio.
For example, increasing the $^6\mathrm{Li}$ isotopic fraction to 0.2 in a 6.25-cm thick coolant region next to the first wall improves the total breeding ratio by about $3\%$ .
Approximately 270 grams of tritium are consumed per day by fusion in a 2250 MW(t) D-T reactor.
Slightly more than this, or approximately 300 grams per day, must be produced and recovered; this corresponds to some 50 moles of $\mathbf{T}_2$ or to 100 moles of TF per day.
In our hypothetical CTR the mean residence time of the fluid in the blanket is 5 minutes per cycle.
Some 0.174 moles of $\mathbf{T}_2$ , or 0.348 moles TF is, accordingly, produced in the $\mathrm{Li_2BeF_4}$ in this interval.

If the fluid entering the blanket contained no tritium species the fluid emerging from the blanket will contain about $1.74 \times 10^{-7}$ moles $\mathbf{T}_2$ (or alternatively about $3.48 \times 10^{-7}$ moles TF) per liter if complete homogeneity is assumed.

Solubility of $\mathbf{H}_2$ in molten $\mathrm{Li_2BeF_4}$ has been shown to increase linearly with pressure of $\mathbf{H}_2$ ; at $1000^{\circ}\mathbf{K}$ , the solubility should be near $7\times 10^{-5}$ moles $\mathbf{H}_2$ per liter of salt per atmosphere of $\mathbf{H}_2$ (18).

If the bred tritium occurs as $\mathbf{T}_2$ , and if the solubility behavior of $\mathbf{T}_2$ and $\mathbf{H}_2$ are similar, the emerging blanket fluid carries $\mathbf{T}_2$ (generated during its pass through the blanket) equivalent to a saturating pressure of about $2.5\times 10^{-3}$ atmospheres.

The solubility of HF in molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ also depends linearly on pressure of the solute gas, and its Henry's law constant is $10^{-2}$ moles HF per liter of salt per atmosphere HF at $1000^{\mathrm{OK}}$ (19).

The TF produced during each cycle of coolant through the blanket region will correspond to about $3.5 \times 10^{-7}$ moles TF per liter of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ ; this is equivalent to a saturation pressure of about $3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ atmospheres of TF.

If, for example, the TF can be allowed to concentrate until its pressure is $10^{-3}$ atmospheres, sparging of perhaps $5\mathrm{ft}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ of the fluid with helium should suffice for effective recovery of the bred tritium.

If we set $N_{NiF_2} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}$ (equivalent to 6 parts per million of $Ni^{2+}$ , a value that seems likely to be tolerable) we calculate pressures of $H_2$ of $6 \times 10^{-9}$ atmospheres and $5 \times 10^{-6}$ atmospheres, respectively, in equilibrium with HF pressures of $3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ and $10^{-3}$ atmospheres.

At this stage in the technology of fusion reactors one should probably not dismiss the possibility of using stainless steel or a chromium-containing nickel-based alloy at temperatures at or below $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ .
Such materials can, perhaps, be coated with molybdenum, tungsten, or nickel, by electrodeposition (22) or by plasma spraying (23).

In our 2250 MW reactor, these reactions yield, respectively, the equivalent of $500\mathrm{g}$ and $70\mathrm{g}$ of fluorine per day.
This problem is generally similar to that encountered in fission of uranium (as $\mathrm{UF}_4$ ) in the MSRE (1);
\mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +}

If, for example, the concentration of cerium in the melt is set at $10^{-4}$ mole fraction the blanket will contain $6 \times 10^{3}$ mole of $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+} + \mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ , and the $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}/\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ ratio would require chemical adjustment on a cycle time of many days.
It must deal with the $\mathbf{F}_2$ generated by transmutation of beryllium but it must also reduce the 100 moles per day of TF produced by transmutation in the LiF.
This nitrogen isotope decays, with a 7.3 sec. half-life, to an oxygen isotope
and the result is probably, although the mechanism may be complex, grow-in of $0^{2-}$ .
The absolute quantity of $^{16}\mathrm{N}$ formed by this reaction is relatively uncertain; it is estimated to be, within a factor of three, 120 grams/day.
The very short half-life of this isotope guarantees that all the $^{16}\mathrm{N}$ decays within the CTR blanket.
The concentration of $^{16}\mathrm{N}$ , in whatever chemical form, within the $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{BeF}_{4}$ cannot exceed 1.1 parts in $10^{11}$ .
If all the $^{16}\mathrm{N}^{-}$ decayed within the blanket salt, the oxide concentration of our hypothetical CTR would increase about 60 parts per billion per day.
For the hypothetical CTR the daily production of helium is about 125 gram atoms or nearly 100 standard cubic feet.
Helium is relatively insoluble in molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ (24); the solubility at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ is $1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ moles He per liter salt per atmosphere.
Helium produced per pass of blanket salt corresponds to a saturation pressure of $2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ atmospheres.
If no sparging were attempted the helium pressure would reach 1 atmosphere in about 30 hours.
As indicated in Table 3 above, LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ in molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ are very stable materials.
Indeed, experience with the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (25) has shown negligible corrosion by this fluid on a nickel-base alloy (Hastelloy N).

However, such salts are excellent fluxes for metallic oxides and halides, and films of such substances afford no protection against oxidizing agents carried by such melts; accordingly, as described above, HF (or TF) may react with the containment metal, and impurity ions such as $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ will react with metallic iron or chromium in the container metal (1).
Melts such as $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ are chemically inert toward, and do not wet, graphite (1).

Similarly, metallic Be cannot react appreciably with $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ (but the Be could certainly react with TF or with the $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}/\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ couple proposed as a redox buffer in the system).

Experience gained in the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (26) and in an extensive in-pile radiation testing program showed that as long as the temperature was greater than $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (27), radiolytic decomposition was of no importance to corrosion of structural metals or graphite.
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (\mathrm {g}) + \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} (\ell) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {B e O} (\mathrm {c}) + 2 \mathrm {H F} (\mathrm {g})

Since BeO is only very slightly soluble (125 ppm at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) in $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ (28), a large in-leakage of steam would soon lead to the precipitation of BeO in the salt circuit.
The molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ can, if necessary, be freed of oxide by treatment at elevated temperatures with anhydrous HF (29).
Table 2 above suggests that LiCl and $\mathrm{Li_2CO_3}$ show, in reasonable (though not optimized) blanket configurations, breeding ratios that are unsatisfactory.
Breeding ratios have also been calculated for $\mathrm{LiNO_2}$ (13) and $\mathrm{LiNO_3}$ (15); the results tend to be quite unfavorable.
Impink (13), for example, obtained the value 0.82 for $\mathrm{LiNO_2}$ .

Lithium oxide $(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{O})$ has a lithium density nearly $50\%$ above that of metallic lithium, but its melting point of nearly $1470^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (6) eliminates it as a major constituent of a blanket fluid.
Lithium chloride melts at $610^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (6) and should be reasonably compatible with CTR metals, but its breeding ratio (see Table 2) appears inferior.
The major drawback of LiF is its melting point of $848^{\circ}C$ .

If the blanket region were operated at very high temperatures $(>900^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , then LiF could be used in conjunction with an intermediate heat-exchange medium--liquid Na, a lower melting salt, or perhaps a boiling alkali metal system.
The melting point of LiF can be substantially lowered by many solutes; the ideal solute should lower the melting point below $374^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Dissolved $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{O}$ should increase the breeding ratio slightly, but considering the probable limited solubility of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{O}$ , the melting temperature (more accurately, liquidus temperature) of LiF will not drop below $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Using $\mathrm{AlF}_3$ and/or another alkali fluoride to lower the melting temperature to $\sim 700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ should not have dire chemical consequences, but the breeding ratio will almost certainly suffer.
The phase diagram for LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ (30), presented in Fig.
1, shows that a melting temperature as low as $363^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is available in this system.

Unfortunately, the viscosity of the melt increases with $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ concentration, and mixtures with $\sim 40$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ have viscosities greater than 50 centipoise at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (31).

The optimum salt mixture of low melting temperature and acceptable viscosity, and with a reasonably good tritium breeding ratio is at $\sim 33$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , corresponding to the $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ used for illustrative purposes in earlier sections of this report.

Decreasing the $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ concentration below 33 mole $\%$ may have a modest beneficial effect upon breeding ratio; this increase might, possibly, offset disadvantages posed by the increased liquidus temperature and likely changes in chemical behavior.

In summary, the answer to the first question posed above--the best blanket coolant salt in which to breed tritium is LiF, but its melting point of $848^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ limits its usefulness only for cooling a blanket that operates above this temperature.
A partial separation of breeding and cooling functions, posed in the second question above, has been approached by Steiner (12).

the tritium breeding ratio for a blanket design in which $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ cooled the vacuum wall and lithium metal assumed the rest of the heat-transfer function, and showed that the breeding ratio (1.22) was $8.3\%$ less than that (1.33) for the same blanket with lithium as the sole coolant.
This small loss in breeding ratio suggests that other salts, especially those melting below $374^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , might also serve as vacuum-wall coolants.
The eutectic mixture of LiCl and KCl, which melts at $354^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (32), might be suitable for such service.

In principle, a complete separation of breeding and cooling functions might be embodied in a blanket design in which a molten salt cools the vacuum wall, a moderately thick region of quiescent lithium metal, and a graphite moderator-reflector.[11] The coolant salt for this application must have great chemical stability (to avoid deleterious destabilization in the magnetic field and to avoid corrosion of the CTR metal), sound heat transfer properties, and, preferably, a low freezing point.
These specifications narrow the choice of major component to fluorides, chlorides, and oxides, of lighter alkali and alkaline earth metals which melt below $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

that, if subsequent calculations show that the breeding ratio does not suffer unduly, the ternary eutectic LiF-NaF-KF (melting point $454^{\circ}C$ ) and the binary eutectic LiCl-KCl may be suitable coolants.

section: MOLTEN SALTS IN LASER-INDUCED FUSION REACTORS
text: Two major uncertainties in use of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ in CTRs such as our hypothetical device stem from (a) potentially troublesome interactions with the large magnetic field and (b) the fact that the first (plasma-containing) wall and blanket structure degrade the neutron spectrum so that the $^7\mathrm{Li}(\mathfrak{n},\alpha \mathfrak{n}^{\prime})\mathrm{T}$ reaction is reduced and tritium breeding becomes marginal.

section: MOLTEN SALTS IN LASER-INDUCED FUSION REACTORS
text: Lubin and Fraas (33) have described a device in which pellets of deuterium and tritium produce a plasma upon ignition by an energy pulse from a suitable laser.
